---
title: "Image formation and modes"
format: html
---

## The Pulse - Echo Principle

Diagnostic ultrasound relies on the **pulse-echo principle**.

-   The probe emits a short pulse of ultrasound and then waits for returning echoes.
-   Ultrasounds are reflected when they hit interfaces
-   When a reflected ultrasound ("echo") reaches the probe, it is converted into an electrical signal by the piezoelectric crystals.

The ultrasound system measures the time elapsed between emission and reception.

Because the speed of sound in soft tissues is assumed to be constant, the depth of the reflecting interface can be calculated:

$$
\text{Depth}
=
\frac{c \times t}{2}
$$

where:

-   $c$ = speed of sound in tissue;
-   $t$ = measured round-trip travel time.

::: callout-note
## Example

Suppose an echo returns after:

$$
t = 130\ \mu s
$$

The depth of the reflecting interface is:

$$
\frac{1540 \times 130\times10^{-6}}{2}
=
0.10\ m
$$

The interface is therefore located approximately **10 cm** from the probe.
:::

![The pulse-echo principle (AI generated)](/images/physics/pusle-echo.png){fig-align="center"}

## Ultrasound Modes

The **mode** describes how the ultrasound system displays the echoes received from tissues. Several imaging modes are available.

-   **A-mode (Amplitude mode):** the earliest ultrasound mode. Echoes are displayed as peaks whose height is proportional to echo intensity. It is now rarely used in clinical practice.
-   **B-mode (Brightness mode or 2D mode):** grayscale image representing a two-dimensional section of the tissues.
-   **M-mode (Motion mode or TM):** representation of tissue motion along a single ultrasound line over time.

Doppler imaging and three-dimensional ultrasound are not covered in this course.

### B-Mode (2D Mode)

Each emitted ultrasound beam explores a single direction through the body.

Along this path, multiple interfaces reflect varying amounts of ultrasound energy.

For each echo received, the machine determines:

1.  The depth of the interface from the travel time.
2.  The brightness of the interface from the echo intensity.

By emitting multiple beams in adjacent directions and assembling the corresponding lines, the ultrasound system reconstructs a two-dimensional representation of the scanned tissues.

The resulting image is the standard grayscale image used for most ultrasound examinations.

![B-mode (AI generated)](/images/physics/b-mode.png){fig-align="center"}

### M-Mode

In M-mode, the ultrasound system repeatedly samples a single ultrasound line over time.

The image displays:

-   time on the horizontal axis;
-   depth on the vertical axis;
-   brightness according to echo intensity.

Structures that remain stationary appear as horizontal lines.

Structures moving toward or away from the probe change depth over time and generate oblique or curved traces.

Structures moving perpendicular to the ultrasound beam may intermittently enter and leave the sampled line, producing discontinuous traces.

M-mode is used mostly to measure variation of lengths over time, or to help identify subtle motion patterns.

![M-mode (AI generated)](/images/physics/m-mode.png)

::: callout-warning
## Key points

-   The ultrasound systems translates received echoes into images, using the pulse echo principle
-   Each echo indicates an interface: the distance between the probe and the interface is estimated from the travel time of the echo
-   The B mode (or 2D) is a two dimensional slice view
-   The M mode (or TM) represents the movement of structurs along one dimension
:::
